readings.title: Computer Organization and Design RISC-V Edition — Patterson and Hennessy
readings.authors: David A. Patterson and John L. Hennessy
readings.note: The standard undergraduate textbook for computer architecture. Chapters 4-5 cover pipelining, hazards, and forwarding. Chapter 5.7 covers out-of-order execution and Tomasulo's algorithm. Use this as the primary reference for midterm review — it covers weeks 5-9 material at the right depth.

readings.title: Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach (6th Edition) — Hennessy and Patterson
readings.authors: John L. Hennessy and David A. Patterson
readings.note: The graduate-level companion. Chapter 3 covers ILP, Tomasulo's algorithm with ROB, speculation, and VLIW in depth. Appendix C covers pipelining fundamentals. This is the definitive reference for the out-of-order execution material in weeks 7-9.

readings.title: CMU 18-447: Introduction to Computer Architecture — Lecture Slides and Problems
readings.note: Professor Mutlu's freely available lecture materials cover all midterm topics. Particularly valuable are his worked examples for Tomasulo's algorithm, cache design, and branch prediction. The problem sets include exam-style questions with solutions.

readings.title: Digital Design and Computer Architecture: RISC-V Edition — Harris and Harris
readings.authors: Sarah L. Harris and David Harris
readings.note: Excellent coverage of the transistor-to-processor path (weeks 1-6). Appendix B has the complete RISC-V RV32I instruction reference. Chapter 7 covers the single-cycle and multi-cycle processor designs. Use for review of the earlier course material.

readings.title: RISC-V ISA Specification (Volume 1, Unprivileged)
readings.note: The official RISC-V specification. Chapter 2 defines the RV32I base integer instruction set including all instruction formats and encodings. Essential reference for any ISA-related exam questions. Freely available.

readings.title: IEEE 754-2019 Standard for Floating-Point Arithmetic
readings.note: The authoritative standard for floating-point representation. Section 3 defines the binary32 (single-precision) format used in week 2 material. Understanding the biased exponent, implicit leading 1, and special values (NaN, infinity, denormals) is essential for the midterm.
